question: B-3. Two cars travel around a track at equal and constant speeds, each completing a lap every hour. From a common starting point, the first starts at time \( t=0 \) and the second at an arbitrary later time \( t=T>0 \). Prove that there is a total period of exactly one hour during the motion in which the first has completed twice as many laps as the second.
solution:
B-3 At time \( t \), car 1 has conpleted [ \( t \) ] laps and car 2 has conpleted [ \( t-T] \) laps. The problem is to find values of \( t \geqq T \) for which \( [t]=2[t-T] \).

Let \( T=k+\delta \), where \( 0 \leqq \delta<1, k \) an integer. Consider any integral interval [ \( m, m+1 \) ] and let \( m \leqq t<m+1 \). Then \( t=m+\varepsilon \), where \( 0 \leqq \varepsilon<1 \). Then the equation to be solved becomes
\[
[t]=m=2[t-T]=2[m+\varepsilon-(k+\delta)]=2[m-k+\varepsilon-\delta] .
\]

Thus \( m=2(m-k) \), if \( \varepsilon \geqq \delta \) and \( m=2(m-k-1) \), if \( \varepsilon<\delta \). If \( 1>\varepsilon \geqq \delta \), then \( m=2 k \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 k+\delta, 2 k+1] \), which has length \( 1-\delta \).

If \( 0 \leqq \varepsilon<\delta \), then \( m=2 k+2 \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 k+2 \), \( 2 k+2+\delta] \) which has length \( \delta \). Therefore the total length is \( 1-\delta+\delta=1 \).

Comment: The problem should have been more explicit by stating "after the start of the second car" instead of "during the mction'. The solution is given for this interpretation, whereas, if \( t<T,[t-T] \) is negative but the second car would have completed zero laps.

descriptive_long.question: B-3. Two cars travel around a track at equal and constant speeds, each completing a lap every hour. From a common starting point, the first starts at time \( timevar=0 \) and the second at an arbitrary later time \( timevar=delaytime>0 \). Prove that there is a total period of exactly one hour during the motion in which the first has completed twice as many laps as the second.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3 At time \( timevar \), car 1 has conpleted [ \( timevar \) ] laps and car 2 has conpleted [ \( timevar-delaytime] \) laps. The problem is to find values of \( timevar \geqq delaytime \) for which \( [timevar]=2[timevar-delaytime] \).

Let \( delaytime=intshift+smalldelta \), where \( 0 \leqq smalldelta<1, intshift \) an integer. Consider any integral interval [ \( lapindex, lapindex+1 \) ] and let \( lapindex \leqq timevar<lapindex+1 \). Then \( timevar=lapindex+smalloffset \), where \( 0 \leqq smalloffset<1 \). Then the equation to be solved becomes
\[
[timevar]=lapindex=2[timevar-delaytime]=2[lapindex+smalloffset-(intshift+smalldelta)]=2[lapindex-intshift+smalloffset-smalldelta] .
\]

Thus \( lapindex=2(lapindex-intshift) \), if \( smalloffset \geqq smalldelta \) and \( lapindex=2(lapindex-intshift-1) \), if \( smalloffset<smalldelta \). If \( 1>smalloffset \geqq smalldelta \), then \( lapindex=2 intshift \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 intshift+smalldelta, 2 intshift+1] \), which has length \( 1-smalldelta \).

If \( 0 \leqq smalloffset<smalldelta \), then \( lapindex=2 intshift+2 \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 intshift+2 \), \( 2 intshift+2+smalldelta] \) which has length \( smalldelta \). Therefore the total length is \( 1-smalldelta+smalldelta=1 \).

Comment: The problem should have been more explicit by stating \"after the start of the second car\" instead of \"during the mction'. The solution is given for this interpretation, whereas, if \( timevar<delaytime,[timevar-delaytime] \) is negative but the second car would have completed zero laps.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-3. Two cars travel around a track at equal and constant speeds, each completing a lap every hour. From a common starting point, the first starts at time \( lanternfish =0 \) and the second at an arbitrary later time \( lanternfish = moonlight >0 \). Prove that there is a total period of exactly one hour during the motion in which the first has completed twice as many laps as the second.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3 At time \( lanternfish \), car 1 has conpleted [ \( lanternfish \) ] laps and car 2 has conpleted [ \( lanternfish - moonlight ] \) laps. The problem is to find values of \( lanternfish \geqq moonlight \) for which \( [ lanternfish ] = 2[ lanternfish - moonlight ] \).

Let \( moonlight = limerick + evergreen \), where \( 0 \leqq evergreen < 1, limerick \) an integer. Consider any integral interval [ \( toothbrush , toothbrush +1 \) ] and let \( toothbrush \leqq lanternfish < toothbrush +1 \). Then \( lanternfish = toothbrush + raspberry \), where \( 0 \leqq raspberry < 1 \). Then the equation to be solved becomes
\[
[ lanternfish ] = toothbrush = 2[ lanternfish - moonlight ] = 2[ toothbrush + raspberry - ( limerick + evergreen ) ] = 2[ toothbrush - limerick + raspberry - evergreen ] .
\]

Thus \( toothbrush = 2( toothbrush - limerick ) \), if \( raspberry \geqq evergreen \) and \( toothbrush = 2( toothbrush - limerick - 1 ) \), if \( raspberry < evergreen \). If \( 1 > raspberry \geqq evergreen \), then \( toothbrush = 2 limerick \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 limerick + evergreen, 2 limerick + 1 ] \), which has length \( 1 - evergreen \).

If \( 0 \leqq raspberry < evergreen \), then \( toothbrush = 2 limerick + 2 \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 limerick + 2, 2 limerick + 2 + evergreen ] \) which has length \( evergreen \). Therefore the total length is \( 1 - evergreen + evergreen = 1 \).

Comment: The problem should have been more explicit by stating "after the start of the second car" instead of "during the mction". The solution is given for this interpretation, whereas, if \( lanternfish < moonlight ,[ lanternfish - moonlight ] \) is negative but the second car would have completed zero laps.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-3. Two cars travel around a track at equal and constant speeds, each completing a lap every hour. From a common starting point, the first starts at time \( timeless=0 \) and the second at an arbitrary later time \( timeless=immediacy>0 \). Prove that there is a total period of exactly one hour during the motion in which the first has completed twice as many laps as the second.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3 At time \( timeless \), car 1 has conpleted [ \( timeless \) ] laps and car 2 has conpleted [ \( timeless-immediacy] \) laps. The problem is to find values of \( timeless \geqq immediacy \) for which \( [timeless]=2[timeless-immediacy] \).

Let \( immediacy=fractional+totality \), where \( 0 \leqq totality<1, fractional \) an integer. Consider any integral interval [ \( limitless, limitless+1 \) ] and let \( limitless \leqq timeless<limitless+1 \). Then \( timeless=limitless+enormity \), where \( 0 \leqq enormity<1 \). Then the equation to be solved becomes
\[
[timeless]=limitless=2[timeless-immediacy]=2[limitless+enormity-(fractional+totality)]=2[limitless-fractional+enormity-totality] .
\]

Thus \( limitless=2(limitless-fractional) \), if \( enormity \geqq totality \) and \( limitless=2(limitless-fractional-1) \), if \( enormity<totality \). If \( 1>enormity \geqq totality \), then \( limitless=2 fractional \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 fractional+totality, 2 fractional+1] \), which has length \( 1-totality \).

If \( 0 \leqq enormity<totality \), then \( limitless=2 fractional+2 \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 fractional+2 \), \( 2 fractional+2+totality] \) which has length \( totality \). Therefore the total length is \( 1-totality+totality=1 \).

Comment: The problem should have been more explicit by stating "after the start of the second car" instead of "during the mction'. The solution is given for this interpretation, whereas, if \( timeless<immediacy,[timeless-immediacy] \) is negative but the second car would have completed zero laps.

garbled_string.question: B-3. Two cars travel around a track at equal and constant speeds, each completing a lap every hour. From a common starting point, the first starts at time \( blarmpqz=0 \) and the second at an arbitrary later time \( blarmpqz=sweltruv>0 \). Prove that there is a total period of exactly one hour during the motion in which the first has completed twice as many laps as the second.
garbled_string.solution:
B-3 At time \( blarmpqz \), car 1 has conpleted [ \( blarmpqz \) ] laps and car 2 has conpleted [ \( blarmpqz-sweltruv] \) laps. The problem is to find values of \( blarmpqz \geqq sweltruv \) for which \( [blarmpqz]=2[blarmpqz-sweltruv] \).

Let \( sweltruv=movtrens+plinxode \), where \( 0 \leqq plinxode<1, movtrens \) an integer. Consider any integral interval [ \( quostnex, quostnex+1 \) ] and let \( quostnex \leqq blarmpqz<quostnex+1 \). Then \( blarmpqz=quostnex+vydricka \), where \( 0 \leqq vydricka<1 \). Then the equation to be solved becomes
\[
[blarmpqz]=quostnex=2[blarmpqz-sweltruv]=2[quostnex+vydricka-(movtrens+plinxode)]=2[quostnex-movtrens+vydricka-plinxode] .
\]

Thus \( quostnex=2(quostnex-movtrens) \), if \( vydricka \geqq plinxode \) and \( quostnex=2(quostnex-movtrens-1) \), if \( vydricka<plinxode \). If \( 1>vydricka \geqq plinxode \), then \( quostnex=2 movtrens \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 movtrens+plinxode, 2 movtrens+1] \), which has length \( 1-plinxode \).

If \( 0 \leqq vydricka<plinxode \), then \( quostnex=2 movtrens+2 \) and the equation is satisfied during [ \( 2 movtrens+2 \), \( 2 movtrens+2+plinxode] \) which has length \( plinxode \). Therefore the total length is \( 1-plinxode+plinxode=1 \).

Comment: The problem should have been more explicit by stating "after the start of the second car" instead of "during the mction'. The solution is given for this interpretation, whereas, if \( blarmpqz<sweltruv,[blarmpqz-sweltruv] \) is negative but the second car would have completed zero laps.

kernel_variant.question:
Let a fixed checkpoint $P$ lie on a circular course that two identical autonomous drones travel at the same constant speed, each completing one full lap every hour.

*  Drone A passes $P$ at the clock time $t=-T$ (with $T>0$) and afterwards continues indefinitely.  
*  Drone B passes $P$ at the clock time $t=0$ and afterwards continues indefinitely.

For every real $t\ge 0$ set  
\[
A(t):=\lfloor t+T\rfloor ,\qquad 
B(t):=\lfloor t\rfloor ,
\]
so that $A(t)$ (resp. $B(t)$) equals the number of completed laps of Drone A (resp. Drone B) by the instant $t$.

Throughout write  
\[
T=k+\delta \quad\text{with}\quad k\in\mathbb Z,\;k\ge 0,\;0\le\delta<1.\tag{$\ast$}
\]

Part (a)  (Prologue --- existence of one contiguous hour)  
Prove that a contiguous time-interval of length exactly one hour on which  
\[
A(t)=2\,B(t)\qquad\text{for every }t\text{ in the interval}
\]
exists if and only if $T$ is an integer (that is, $\delta=0$ in $(\ast)$).

Part (b)  (Complete description of all solutions for the ratio $2:1$)  
For the representation $(\ast)$ determine the set  
\[
S:=\{\,t\ge 0 : A(t)=2\,B(t)\,\}.
\]
Show that  

(i)  If $\delta=0$ then\; $S=[k,k+1)$ (a single interval of length $1$).  

(ii) If $0<\delta<1$ then 
\[
S=S_{1}\cup S_{2},\qquad 
S_{1}=[\,k,\;k+1-\delta),\;
S_{2}=[\,k+2-\delta,\;k+2),
\]
so $|S_{1}|=1-\delta$, $|S_{2}|=\delta$ and $|S|=1$.

Part (c)  (Total measure and asymptotic density for the ratio $2:1$)  
Prove that for every $R>0$  
\[
|S\cap[0,R]|\le 1,
\qquad
\lim_{R\to\infty}\frac{|S\cap[0,R]|}{R}=0.
\]

Part (d)  (General integral ratio --- harder extension)  
Fix an integer $r\ge 2$ and keep the notation $A(t)=\lfloor t+T\rfloor$, $B(t)=\lfloor t\rfloor$.  
Define  
\[
S_{r}:=\{\,t\ge 0 : A(t)=r\,B(t)\,\}.
\]

Prove the following complete description for all $T>0$.

*  Put $k$ and $\delta$ as in $(\ast)$.  Then $S_{r}\neq\varnothing$ if and only if  
\[
\bigl(k\equiv 0\pmod{r-1}\bigr)\quad\text{or}\quad
\bigl(k\equiv -1\pmod{r-1}\ \text{ and }\ \delta>0\bigr).
\]

*  If $k\equiv 0\pmod{r-1}$ and $T=k+\delta$ (with any $0\le\delta<1$) then  
\[
S_{r}=\Bigl[\,\dfrac{k}{\,r-1\,},\; \dfrac{k}{\,r-1\,}+1-\delta\Bigr),
\qquad |S_{r}|=1-\delta.
\]

*  If $k\equiv -1\pmod{r-1}$ and $\delta>0$ then  
\[
S_{r}=\Bigl[\;\dfrac{k+1}{\,r-1\,}+1-\delta,\; \dfrac{k+1}{\,r-1\,}+1\Bigr),
\qquad |S_{r}|=\delta.
\]
(When $\delta=0$ this interval degenerates and $S_{r}$ is empty.)

*  For $r=2$ one has $r-1=1$, so every $k$ satisfies both congruence conditions.  
   Consequently  
   \[
   S_{2}= \begin{cases}
           [\,k,k+1)\;, &\delta=0,\\[4pt]
           [\,k,k+1-\delta)\cup[\,k+2-\delta,k+2), &0<\delta<1,
          \end{cases}
   \qquad |S_{2}|=1.
   \]

In all cases $|S_{r}|\le 1$, and therefore  
\[
\forall r\ge 2:\qquad 
\lim_{R\to\infty}\frac{|S_{r}\cap[0,R]|}{R}=0.
\]

(Here $|\;\cdot\;|$ denotes Lebesgue measure.)


------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Preliminaries.  
Keep the notation $(\ast)$ and write every $t\ge 0$ uniquely as  
\[
t=m+\varepsilon ,\qquad m\in\mathbb Z,\ m\ge 0,\ 0\le\varepsilon<1.\tag{1}
\]
Then  
\[
A(t)=\lfloor t+T\rfloor =\lfloor m+\varepsilon+k+\delta\rfloor
      =m+k+\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor,\tag{2}
\qquad
B(t)=\lfloor t\rfloor =m.\tag{3}
\]

Because $0\le\varepsilon,\delta<1$,
\[
\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor=
\begin{cases}
0,&\varepsilon<1-\delta,\\[4pt]
1,&\varepsilon\ge 1-\delta \ (\text{which implies }\delta>0).
\end{cases}\tag{4}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a)
------------------------------------------------------------------
($\Rightarrow$)  Suppose an interval $J$ of length $1$ with $A=2B$ exists.  
If $\delta\neq 0$ the jump points of $A$ are the non-integers $\mathbb Z-(k+\delta)$, while the jump points of $B$ are the integers $\mathbb Z$.  
Because these sets are disjoint, $A$ and $B$ cannot jump simultaneously.  
Consequently $A-2B$ changes at every jump point of either function, so $A=2B$ cannot hold on an interval of length $1$.  Thus $\delta\neq 0$ is impossible, hence $\delta=0$.

($\Leftarrow$)  If $\delta=0$ then (2)-(3) give $A(t)=m+k$ and $B(t)=m$.  
The equality $A=2B$ reduces to $m=k$, with $\varepsilon$ arbitrary, so the equality holds exactly on $[k,k+1)$, an interval of length $1$. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b)
------------------------------------------------------------------
Insert (4) into (2)-(3) for the ratio $2:1$:
\[
m+k+\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor=2m
\ \Longleftrightarrow\
m=k+\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor.\tag{5}
\]

Case 1: $\delta=0$.  
Then $\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor=0$ for all $\varepsilon$, so (5) forces $m=k$ and $t=m+\varepsilon\in[k,k+1)$.  Thus $S=[k,k+1)$.

Case 2: $0<\delta<1$.  
If $\varepsilon<1-\delta$ then $\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor=0$ and (5) gives $m=k$, hence  
$t\in[k,k+1-\delta)=S_{1}$.  
If $\varepsilon\ge 1-\delta$ then $\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor=1$ and (5) gives $m=k+1$, hence  
$t\in[k+2-\delta,k+2)=S_{2}$.  
The two intervals are disjoint and exhaust $S$, with total length $1$. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (c)
------------------------------------------------------------------
In every case $S$ is either one interval of length $1$ or two disjoint intervals whose total length is $1$.  Therefore $|S\cap[0,R]|\le 1$ for every $R>0$, whence  
\[
\lim_{R\to\infty}\frac{|S\cap[0,R]|}{R}=0. \qquad\quad\Box
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (d) (Integral ratio $r\ge 2$)
------------------------------------------------------------------
Let $r\ge 2$ be fixed.  From (2)-(3) we require
\[
m+k+\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor=r\,m
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
(r-1)m=k+\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor.\tag{6}
\]

Put  
\[
s:=\lfloor\varepsilon+\delta\rfloor\in\{0,1\}.\tag{7}
\]
Then (6) becomes  
\[
(r-1)m=k+s.\tag{8}
\]

Because $m$ must be an integer, $k+s$ must be divisible by $r-1$; conversely, whenever $k+s\equiv 0\pmod{r-1}$, (8) yields a suitable $m$.  Two sub-cases arise.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Case A: $s=0$  (this needs no restriction on $\delta$)
------------------------------------------------------------------
The divisibility condition is  
\[
k\equiv 0\pmod{r-1}.\tag{9A}
\]
When (9A) holds, (8) gives $m=k/(r-1)$.  
Because $s=0$ corresponds to $\varepsilon<1-\delta$ (see (4)), we obtain
\[
S_{r}^{(0)}=
\Bigl[\,\frac{k}{\,r-1\,},\;\frac{k}{\,r-1\,}+1-\delta\Bigr),
\qquad |S_{r}^{(0)}|=1-\delta.\tag{10A}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------
Case B: $s=1$  (possible only if $\delta>0$)
------------------------------------------------------------------
Now $k+s=k+1$ must satisfy  
\[
k\equiv -1\pmod{r-1},\tag{9B}
\]
and $\varepsilon\ge 1-\delta$ by (4), which forces $\delta>0$.  
When both conditions hold, (8) gives $m=(k+1)/(r-1)$, and therefore
\[
S_{r}^{(1)}=
\Bigl[\,\frac{k+1}{\,r-1\,}+1-\delta,\;\frac{k+1}{\,r-1\,}+1\Bigr),
\qquad |S_{r}^{(1)}|=\delta.\tag{10B}
\]
If $\delta=0$ the interval in (10B) collapses and $S_{r}^{(1)}$ is empty.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary
------------------------------------------------------------------
Thus
\[
S_{r}=
\begin{cases}
S_{r}^{(0)}, & k\equiv 0\pmod{r-1},\\[6pt]
S_{r}^{(1)}, & k\equiv -1\pmod{r-1}\text{ and }\delta>0,\\[6pt]
\varnothing, &\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}\tag{11}
\]
Consequently $|S_{r}|\le 1$ in every case.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Specialisation $r=2$
------------------------------------------------------------------
Here $r-1=1$, so $k\equiv 0\pmod{1}$ and $k\equiv -1\pmod{1}$ are both automatically true.  From (10A)-(10B):

*  If $\delta=0$ only Case A contributes, giving  
   \[
   S_{2}=[\,k,k+1),\qquad |S_{2}|=1.
   \]

*  If $0<\delta<1$ both cases contribute and  
   \[
   S_{2}=[\,k,k+1-\delta)\cup[\,k+2-\delta,k+2),\qquad |S_{2}|=1.
   \]

------------------------------------------------------------------
Density estimate
------------------------------------------------------------------
Because $S_{r}$ is either empty, one interval of length $\le 1$, or (for $r=2$ with $\delta>0$) two disjoint intervals whose total length is $1$, we have $|S_{r}\cap[0,R]|\le 1$ for every $R>0$.  Hence
\[
\forall r\ge 2:\qquad
\lim_{R\to\infty}\frac{|S_{r}\cap[0,R]|}{R}=0.\qquad\quad\Box
\]

original_kernel_variant.question:
Two identical autonomous drones fly around the same circular course at a constant speed, completing one full lap every hour.

*  Drone A passes the fixed checkpoint P at the clock time t = -T  (with T > 0) and afterwards continues indefinitely.  
*  Drone B passes P at the clock time t = 0 and afterwards continues indefinitely.

For every real t \geq  0 define  
  A(t) := \lfloor t+T\rfloor  and B(t) := \lfloor t\rfloor ,  
so that A(t)   (resp. B(t)) is the number of completed laps of Drone A (resp. B) by the instant t.

Throughout write  
  T = k + \delta  with k \in  \mathbb{Z}, k \geq  0, 0 \leq  \delta  < 1.    (*)

Part (a) (Prologue --- existence of one contiguous hour)  
Prove that a contiguous time-interval of length exactly one hour on which  
   A(t) = 2 B(t)  for every instant t in the interval  
exists if and only if T is an integer (i.e. \delta  = 0 in (*)).

Part (b) (Complete description of all solutions for the ratio 2 : 1)  
For the representation (*) determine the set  
  S := { t \geq  0 : A(t) = 2 B(t) }.  
Show that

 (i) If \delta  = 0 then S = [k, k+1).               (a single interval of length 1)

 (ii) If 0 < \delta  < 1 then S is the disjoint union of the two intervals  
   S_1 = [k, k+1-\delta ) and S_2 = [k+2-\delta , k+2),  
whose lengths are 1-\delta  and \delta , respectively.  

In every case |S| = 1.

Part (c) (Total measure and asymptotic density for the ratio 2 : 1)  
Prove that, for every R > 0,  
  |S \cap  [0,R]| \leq  1, and hence lim_{R\to \infty } |S \cap  [0,R]| / R = 0.

Part (d) (General integral ratio --- corrected harder extension)  
Fix an integer r \geq  2 and keep the notation A(t) = \lfloor t+T\rfloor , B(t) = \lfloor t\rfloor .  
Define  
  S_r := { t \geq  0 : A(t) = r B(t) }.  

Prove the following complete description for all T > 0:

* Put k and \delta  as in (*).  The set S_r is non-empty if and only if  
 k \equiv  0 or k \equiv  -1 \equiv  r-2 (mod r-1).

* If k \equiv  0 (mod r-1) and T = k+\delta  then  
  S_r = [ k/(r-1), k/(r-1) + 1 - \delta  ),  |S_r| = 1 - \delta .

* If k \equiv  -1 (mod r-1) and T = k+\delta  then  
  S_r = [ (k+1)/(r-1) + 1 - \delta , (k+1)/(r-1) + 1 ),  |S_r| = \delta .

* For r = 2 one has r-1 = 1, so both congruence conditions are always satisfied; hence  
  S_2 = [k, k+1-\delta ) \cup  [k+2-\delta , k+2),  |S_2| = 1.

Consequently, for every integer r \geq  2,  
 |S_r| \leq  1 and lim_{R\to \infty } |S_r \cap  [0,R]| / R = 0.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Preliminaries.  
Retain the notation (*) and write every t \geq  0 uniquely as  
  t = m + \varepsilon  with m \in  \mathbb{Z}, m \geq  0, 0 \leq  \varepsilon  < 1.     (1)

Then  
 A(t) = \lfloor t+T\rfloor  = \lfloor m+\varepsilon +k+\delta \rfloor  = m + k + \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor ,             (2)  
 B(t) = \lfloor t\rfloor  = m.                                         (3)

Because 0 \leq  \varepsilon ,\delta  < 1,  
  \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  = 0  iff \varepsilon  < 1-\delta ,         (4a)  
  \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  = 1  iff \varepsilon  \geq  1-\delta .        (4b)



------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a)
------------------------------------------------------------------
(\Rightarrow ) Suppose a one-hour interval J exists on which A = 2 B.  
If \delta  \neq  0 then the ``jump points'' of A are the set \mathbb{Z} - T = \mathbb{Z} - (k+\delta ), i.e. the non-integers \ldots , -\delta , 1-\delta , 2-\delta , \ldots .  
The jump points of B are the integers \mathbb{Z}.  
Because \delta  \notin  \mathbb{Z}, the two jump sets are disjoint; hence A and B can never jump simultaneously.  
Within any open sub-interval of J that contains no jump point both A and B are constant, so equality persists.  
When J meets a jump point of either function equality is destroyed, contradicting the assumed length |J| = 1.  
Therefore \delta  \neq  0 is impossible; hence \delta  = 0.

(\Leftarrow )  If \delta  = 0 then (2)-(3) give A(t) = m+k and B(t) = m.  
Equality A = 2 B becomes m+k = 2m, i.e. m = k, and \varepsilon  is unrestricted.  
Thus A = 2 B exactly on [k,k+1), an interval of length 1. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b)
------------------------------------------------------------------
Insert (4) into (2)-(3) (with r = 2):

 m + k + \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  = 2m  \Leftrightarrow   m = k + \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor .             (5)

Case 1: \delta  = 0.  
Then \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  = 0 for all \varepsilon , so (5) forces m = k and hence  
 t = m+\varepsilon  \in  [k, k+1).  
Therefore S = [k,k+1).

Case 2: 0 < \delta  < 1.  
Using (4a)-(4b) in (5) one obtains

 * If \varepsilon  < 1-\delta   (so \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  = 0) then m = k, giving  
  t \in  [k, k+1-\delta ).  

 * If \varepsilon  \geq  1-\delta  (so \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  = 1) then m = k+1, giving  
  t \in  [k+2-\delta , k+2).

The two intervals are disjoint and exhaust S; their lengths are 1-\delta  and \delta , so |S| = 1. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (c)
------------------------------------------------------------------
In every case S is the union of at most two intervals whose total length is 1.  
Hence for every R > 0 we have |S \cap  [0,R]| \leq  1 and therefore  
  lim_{R\to \infty } |S \cap  [0,R]| / R = 0. \blacksquare   



------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (d) (Integral ratio r \geq  2)
------------------------------------------------------------------
Let r \geq  2 be fixed.  With (1)-(4) we require

 m + k + \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  = r m  \Leftrightarrow   (r-1)m = k + \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor .     (6)

Put  
  s := \lfloor \varepsilon +\delta \rfloor  \in  {0,1}.                             (7)

Then (6) becomes  
  (r-1)m = k + s.                                (8)

For m to be integral we must have  

  k + s \equiv  0 (mod r-1).                           (9)

Because s = 0 or 1, exactly the following two congruence classes can occur:

 * s = 0 \Rightarrow  k \equiv  0 (mod r-1),                     (10a)  
 * s = 1 \Rightarrow  k \equiv  -1 \equiv  r-2 (mod r-1).             (10b)

No other values of k permit solutions, so S_r = \emptyset  unless (10a) or (10b) is satisfied.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Case A: k \equiv  0 (mod r-1) (s = 0).
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Then (8) gives m = k/(r-1), an integer.  
Condition (4a) (\varepsilon  < 1-\delta ) is equivalent to (7) with s = 0, so

  t = m + \varepsilon  \in  [ k/(r-1), k/(r-1) + 1 - \delta  ).     (11)

Set  
  S_r^{(0)} := [ k/(r-1), k/(r-1) + 1 - \delta  ), |S_r^{(0)}| = 1-\delta .   (12)

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Case B: k \equiv  -1 (mod r-1) (s = 1).
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Now (8) gives m = (k+1)/(r-1).  
Condition (4b) (\varepsilon  \geq  1-\delta ) combines with (7) to give

  t = m + \varepsilon  \in  [ m + 1 - \delta , m + 1 )  
    = [ (k+1)/(r-1) + 1 - \delta , (k+1)/(r-1) + 1 ).     (13)

Set  
  S_r^{(1)} := [ (k+1)/(r-1) + 1 - \delta , (k+1)/(r-1) + 1 ),  
  |S_r^{(1)}| = \delta .                                             (14)

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Case C: k \equiv  2,\ldots ,r-3 (mod r-1) (possible only for r \geq  4).
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Neither value of s makes k+s divisible by r-1, so S_r = \emptyset .        (15)

Putting the pieces together,

 S_r = 
   S_r^{(0)}                           if (10a) holds and (10b) fails,  
   S_r^{(1)}                           if (10b) holds and (10a) fails,  
   S_r^{(0)} \cup  S_r^{(1)} (= two intervals) if r = 2.                (16)

Lengths.  Each of S_r^{(0)}, S_r^{(1)} has length \leq  1, and their intersection is empty.  Therefore |S_r| \leq  1 in all cases; equality holds only for r = 2 or when \delta  = 0 with k \equiv  0 (mod r-1).

Density.  Because S_r is either empty, a single interval of length \leq  1, or (when r = 2) two disjoint intervals whose total length is 1, we have |S_r \cap  [0,R]| \leq  1 for every R > 0.  Hence  
  lim_{R\to \infty } |S_r \cap  [0,R]| / R = 0. \blacksquare
